hero_title: Business Days Calculator and Work Schedule Simulator
hero_subtitle: Calculate precise deadlines with national, state, and municipal holidays across Brazil. Ideal for banking, legal, and business deadlines, plus free simulations of Brazilian CLT schedules like 6x1, 5x2, 12x36, and 24x48.
feat_precise_desc: Business and calendar days between two dates, including national holidays.
feat_cal_desc: View annual or monthly calendar with national holidays highlighted.
feat_scale_desc: Easily simulate work schedules: 6x1, 5x2, 12x36, 24x48, and more.
feat_city_desc: Check municipal holidays by city using official city hall links.
feat_report_desc: Print calendars and calculations with a clean layout. Export as CSV and ICS.
calendar_intro_copy: Calculate precise deadlines considering Brazil's national holidays and organize work schedules in a simple, fast, free way.
calendar_guide_title: {year} calendar with national holidays and business days
calendar_guide_p1: The {year} calendar with national holidays makes it easier to plan deadlines, schedules, and deliveries throughout the year. With monthly and annual views, you can quickly spot periods with more days off, extended holidays, and weeks with fewer business days. This kind of reading is valuable for administrative teams, operations, retail, and services because it helps anticipate schedule adjustments and distribute activities more effectively. Instead of checking different sources, you centralize the calendar and business-day review in a single workflow.
calendar_combo_title: How to combine the calendar, business day counter, and schedule simulator
calendar_combo_p1: After locating the period on the calendar, you can use the business day counter to validate deadlines between dates more accurately. This combination reduces rework in contracts, internal schedules, and service commitments. For on-call operations, the next step is to project coverage with the 12x36, 6x1, and on-call schedule simulator, aligning the team's journey with national holidays. That way, planning moves beyond a visual check and becomes a more objective operational decision, with lower risk of conflict between deadlines, demand, and availability.
faq_a1: Enter the start and end dates. The system automatically deducts weekends and holidays to provide an accurate result.
faq_q2: What counts as a business day for banking and legal deadlines?
faq_a2: Business days are Monday through Friday, excluding official holidays.
faq_q3: Which holidays are considered in the calculation?
faq_a3: Official national holidays, state holidays, and municipal holidays based on the selected city's IBGE code.
faq_a4: Optional holidays (pontos facultativos) are not mandatory national holidays. By default, they are not included in the calculation.
faq_q5: Can I calculate business days by state?
faq_a5: Not at the moment. The calculation considers only national holidays.
faq_q6: Does the calculation work for dates that cross year boundaries?
faq_a6: Yes. The calculation works even when the period spans different years.
calc_add_explain: Using the start date as reference, you get the final date by adding X business days.
calc_intro_copy: Enter the period to calculate business days considering national holidays.
calc_picker_hint: You can type dates or pick them directly on the calendar. The result updates in real time.
calc_sat_workday: Saturday is not a business day
calc_rule_weekend_fixed: Saturday and Sunday are not considered business days.

calc_how_item_weekends: Saturday and Sunday are not considered business days.
calc_how_item_optional: Optional holidays are not included in the calculation.
calc_guide_title: How to calculate business days between dates accurately
calc_guide_p1: To calculate business days between dates reliably, the best approach is to consider the full period and automatically deduct Saturdays, Sundays, and national holidays.
calc_guide_p2: The tool also helps in forecast scenarios, when you need to find the final date after adding a specific number of business days.

scale_guide_p1: The schedule simulator was created to turn a complex operational task into a faster and more predictable process. By entering the month, team size, and minimum daily demand, you can view the distribution of work and time off in a clear calendar, with coverage and shortfall highlighted. This helps supervisors and HR teams compare scenarios before publishing the schedule, reducing last-minute adjustments and shift conflicts. Because the planning follows the monthly routine, it becomes easier to align staff availability with service goals and operational continuity.
scale_guide_title_2: How to combine schedules, calendar, and business days
scale_guide_p2: Calculate precise deadlines considering Brazil's national holidays and organize work schedules in a simple, fast, free way. By combining the simulator with the calendar and business day counter, you can anticipate holidays, distribute shifts better, and reduce conflicts between deadlines, demand, and availability.

home_seo_title: Business days counter, holiday calendar and schedule simulator
home_seo_p1: Datas Úteis brings together, in a single environment, what companies, HR professionals, accounting firms and operational teams use most in their daily routine: business days calculation between dates, calendar with national holidays and deadline counter with fast updates. Instead of building manual spreadsheets, you enter the dates and get the result already considering Saturdays, Sundays and national holidays in Brazil.
home_seo_p2: Beyond deadline calculations, the site also supports team planning with the online work schedule simulator, including scenarios like 12x36, 6x1 and on-call schedules. This way, you can check the calendar, validate business days and organize operational coverage in the same workflow.
footer_about: Datas Úteis is a free tool for calculating business days in Brazil, checking calendars with national holidays and using an online work schedule simulator.

blog_scales_clt_article_html:
Work Schedules under Brazil's CLT: Complete Guide

The information below refers to Brazilian labor law (CLT). This content is informational and does not replace professional legal advice.

Organizing work schedules is one of the most important tasks for companies that operate with shifts, duty rosters, or non-standard working hours. Knowing the main models used in Brazil helps employers and workers plan routines better, ensure proper rest, and comply with legal obligations.

What is a work schedule

A work schedule is the organized distribution of the days and times when each employee must work and rest. The goal is to ensure the operation has enough coverage while workers still receive proper rest under the law.

Defining the schedule takes into account factors such as the nature of the activity, the number of available employees, the need for continuous operation, and the collective rules that apply to the category.

What the CLT says about working hours and schedules

Brazil's Consolidation of Labor Laws (CLT) sets general limits for working hours, such as the standard schedule of 8 hours per day and 44 hours per week. It also guarantees paid weekly rest, preferably on Sundays.

The law also allows collective bargaining agreements to define specific working conditions, including alternative schedule models. That is why it is important to review the collective agreement for the category before setting a schedule.

Most common schedule types

  • 5x2 - the employee works 5 consecutive days and rests 2. It is the most traditional model, common in offices and in businesses that operate Monday through Friday.
  • 6x1 - the employee works 6 days and rests 1. It is widely used in retail and services that operate on Saturdays. The rest day must periodically fall on a Sunday, according to the applicable rules. Learn more about the 6x1 schedule.
  • 12x36 - the professional works 12 hours and rests 36 hours. It is common in hospitals, security, and industries that operate 24 hours a day. Learn more about the 12x36 schedule.
  • 24x48 - the employee works 24 consecutive hours followed by 48 hours of rest. This model is more common in specific categories, such as firefighters and some security forces. This type of schedule requires extra attention to legal limits and collective agreements.

How to choose the right schedule

Choosing the ideal work schedule depends on multiple factors: type of activity, need for continuous operation, applicable law, and collective agreements. There is no single model that fits every company.

To make planning easier, you can use the Datas Uteis work schedule simulator. It lets you build schedules visually, compare coverage scenarios, and identify days with staffing shortfalls.

Frequently asked questions

What are the most common work schedule types?

The most common types are 5x2, 6x1, 12x36, and 24x48. Each model has specific rules on working hours, rest, and compensation.

Does the CLT define which schedule a company must use?

The CLT sets working-hour limits and rest rules, but the schedule model depends on the activity and may be defined by an agreement or collective bargaining convention.

Is Sunday time off mandatory?

As a rule, paid weekly rest should fall on a Sunday at least once within the period defined by the applicable law and collective agreements.

blog_scale_12x36_article_html:
12x36 Schedule: How It Works in Practice

The information below refers to Brazilian labor law (CLT). This content is informational and does not replace professional legal advice.

The 12x36 schedule is one of the most common working-time models in activities that require continuous operation, such as hospitals, gatehouses, private security, and industrial plants. In this article, we explain how it works in practice and what Brazilian labor law generally provides.

What the 12x36 schedule is

In a 12x36 schedule, the worker performs a 12-hour shift and then rests for 36 hours before returning to work. This model allows uninterrupted coverage with rotating teams.

Here is a practical example: if the worker starts at 7 a.m., the shift runs until 7 p.m. The next shift would start only at 7 a.m. after the rest period, that is, 36 hours later.

How it works in practice

In practice, the 12x36 schedule alternates workdays and time off continuously. In a typical week, the worker may work on alternating days, which usually means around 3 to 4 working days per week.

This model is useful for operations that need constant presence because it reduces the need for frequent shift changes. However, it requires attention to worker health because of the longer shift length.

What the CLT generally provides

Brazil's labor reform, Law 13,467/2017, expressly allowed the adoption of the 12x36 schedule through an individual written agreement, collective bargaining convention, or collective labor agreement. Before that change, the model generally depended on collective instruments.

In general terms, the law treats the compensation of working hours in the 12x36 schedule as occurring within the work-and-rest cycle itself. This means there is usually no overtime premium simply because the shift lasts 12 hours.

Holidays, rest, and practical notes

Work performed on holidays in a 12x36 schedule is generally compensated by the 36-hour rest period. Even so, collective agreements may set specific conditions, such as double pay or additional compensatory rest.

As for the night shift differential, if part of the shift takes place during the night period, usually from 10 p.m. to 5 a.m. in the urban context, the worker is entitled to the corresponding premium under the applicable rules.

Frequently asked questions

Is the 12x36 schedule allowed under the CLT?

Yes. Brazil's 2017 labor reform allowed the 12x36 schedule through an individual written agreement, collective bargaining convention, or collective labor agreement.

Does a 12x36 worker have the right to a night shift differential?

Usually yes. If part of the shift takes place during the night period, the worker is entitled to the night premium under the applicable rules.

How does the 12x36 schedule work on holidays?

In general, holiday work in a 12x36 schedule is already considered compensated by the 36-hour rest period. However, collective agreements may establish specific conditions.

Simulate your 12x36 schedule

blog_scale_6x1_article_html:
6x1 Schedule: How It Works Day to Day

The information below refers to Brazilian labor law (CLT). This content is informational and does not replace professional legal advice.

The 6x1 schedule is one of the most common working-time models in retail, services, and operations that run on Saturdays. In this article, you will understand how it works, what the worker's rights are, and the main points that deserve attention.

What the 6x1 schedule is

In a 6x1 schedule, the worker performs 6 consecutive workdays and then has 1 day off. Total weekly hours must stay within the legal limit, usually 44 hours per week spread across 6 days.

This model is very common in activities that operate Monday through Saturday, such as stores, supermarkets, restaurants, and service providers.

How it works day to day

In day-to-day practice, the 6x1 schedule works on a rotating basis: the worker performs duties for 6 consecutive days and rests on the seventh. The daily shift is often 7 hours and 20 minutes so that the weekly total remains within the 44-hour limit.

In many cases, the Saturday shift is shorter, which helps keep the weekly workload balanced. The exact arrangement depends on the company and the collective agreement that applies to the category.

Weekly rest and Sundays

The law guarantees workers the right to paid weekly rest, preferably on Sundays. In a 6x1 schedule, the day off may fall on different weekdays, but it must coincide with a Sunday periodically, according to the applicable rules.

In sectors such as retail and essential services, legal and regulatory rules allow Sunday work as long as time off is granted within the required periods.

Points of attention for companies and workers

  • Review the collective agreement for the category to understand any specific rules for the 6x1 schedule.
  • Track daily and weekly hours to avoid excessive working time.
  • Make sure Sunday rest days happen with the required frequency.
  • There is public and legislative debate in Brazil about working-time models. Follow relevant legal updates for your activity.

Frequently asked questions

Is the 6x1 schedule legal?

Yes. The 6x1 schedule is recognized under Brazilian labor law. The worker performs 6 days and rests 1, while respecting the 44-hour weekly limit.

In a 6x1 schedule, can the day off fall on any day?

As a rule, the day off should coincide with a Sunday periodically, according to the applicable law and the category's collective agreements.

Can the 6x1 schedule change in the future?

There is public and legislative debate in Brazil about working-time models, but for now the 6x1 schedule remains valid under current law.

blog_business_days_article_html:
Business Days: What They Are and How to Count Them

Knowing what a business day is and how to count it is essential when calculating banking, legal, labor, and delivery deadlines. In this article, we explain the concept, show the difference between business days and calendar days, and indicate how to use that information in practice.

What business days are

Business days are the days when most companies, banks, and public agencies operate normally. In general, business days run from Monday to Friday, excluding national holidays.

Optional observances, such as Carnival and Corpus Christi, are not mandatory national holidays in Brazil and may or may not be treated as business days depending on the company or public body involved.

Difference between business days and calendar days

Calendar days include every day on the calendar, from Monday through Sunday, including holidays. Business days exclude Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays. This difference directly affects deadline calculations.

For example, a 10-calendar-day deadline starting on a Monday ends on the Wednesday of the following week. A 10-business-day deadline starting on the same date can take up to two and a half weeks, depending on the holidays in the period.

How to count business days

To count business days between two dates, follow these steps:

  • Identify the start date and the end date of the period.
  • Exclude Saturdays and Sundays.
  • Exclude national holidays that fall within the period.
  • The result is the number of business days.

To make it easier, use the Datas Uteis business day calculator. It performs the calculation automatically, considering national holidays.

Where this is used in practice

  • Banking deadlines - boleto settlement, transfers, and investments follow business days.
  • Legal deadlines - many procedural deadlines are counted in business days.
  • Deliveries and logistics - delivery times from postal and transportation companies usually consider business days.
  • Payroll - the fifth business day is a reference date for salary payments in Brazil.
  • Contracts and SLAs - contractual clauses often define deadlines in business days.

Frequently asked questions

Is Saturday a business day?

It depends on the context. For banking calculations and in most deadline calculations, Saturday is not considered a business day.

Are holidays business days?

No. National holidays are excluded from business day counting.

How do I count business days between two dates?

Count all days in the period and subtract Saturdays, Sundays, and national holidays. The Datas Uteis calculator does this automatically.

Calculate business days now

Use the Datas Uteis calculator to count business days between dates with national holidays included.

blog_fifth_business_day_article_html:
Fifth Business Day: What It Is and Why It Matters

The fifth business day is one of the most searched references for workers, accountants, and HR professionals in Brazil. As a rule, it is the deadline used for salary payments in most companies.

What the fifth business day is

The fifth business day is the 5th business day of the month, counted from day 1. The count excludes Saturdays, Sundays, and national holidays. Because of that, the exact date changes from month to month.

When a month starts with a holiday or a weekend, the fifth business day can shift significantly. For example, in January, when day 1 is a holiday, New Year's Day, the count begins on day 2.

Why it matters

Brazil's CLT states that wages must be paid by the 5th business day of the month following the month worked. That is why the fifth business day became an important reference for:

  • Workers planning payments and personal expenses.
  • HR and finance teams organizing payroll flow.
  • Accountants who need to verify payment deadlines.

How to identify the fifth business day

To identify the fifth business day of any month:

  • Start with day 1 of the month.
  • Count only Monday through Friday.
  • Exclude national holidays that fall within the period.
  • The 5th counted day is the fifth business day.

To make it easier, use the Datas Uteis business day calculator in add business days mode. You can also check the calendar with national holidays to identify it visually.

Frequently asked questions

What is the fifth business day?

The fifth business day is the 5th business day of the month counted from day 1. It is the most common salary payment deadline in Brazil.

Does the fifth business day change from month to month?

Yes. Because the count excludes Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays, the fifth business day may fall on different dates every month.

Can I use the calculator to identify the fifth business day?

Yes. Just use the add business days mode starting from day 1 of the month.

Find the fifth business day

Use the Datas Uteis calculator or calendar to identify the fifth business day of any month.

blog_night_additional_article_html:
Night Shift Differential: What It Is and How It Works

The information below refers to Brazilian labor law (CLT). This content is informational and does not replace professional legal advice.

The night shift differential is one of the most relevant rights for workers who perform duties at night. In this article, we explain what it is, which period is considered night work, the concept of the reduced night hour, and how it relates to work schedules.

What the night shift differential is

The night shift differential is an additional percentage paid on top of the worker's pay for work performed during the night period. It exists because night work is considered more demanding and places greater biological strain on the body.

For urban workers in Brazil, the minimum night shift differential is generally 20% over the daytime hourly rate under the CLT. Collective agreements may establish higher percentages.

Urban night period

For urban workers, the night period generally runs from 10 p.m. on one day to 5 a.m. on the next day. Hours worked outside that interval do not trigger the night differential unless there is an extension of the night shift.

For rural workers, the schedule is different: in agriculture, from 9 p.m. to 5 a.m.; in livestock, from 8 p.m. to 4 a.m. This article focuses on the urban context.

Reduced night hour

An important concept related to the night shift differential is the reduced night hour. In the urban context, each night hour is counted as 52 minutes and 30 seconds rather than 60 minutes.

In practice, this means 7 hours of night work correspond to 8 daytime hours for working-time calculation purposes. This reduction recognizes the greater strain of working at night.

Relationship with work schedules

The night shift differential is especially relevant for workers on schedules such as 12x36, where part or all of the shift often occurs during the night period.

To build and view work schedules that involve night periods, use the Datas Uteis work schedule simulator.

Frequently asked questions

What is the night shift differential?

It is an additional amount paid on top of the worker's remuneration for work performed during the night period under Brazilian labor law.

What is the urban night period?

For urban workers, the night period generally runs from 10 p.m. on one day to 5 a.m. on the next day.

What is a reduced night hour?

In the urban context, a night hour is counted as 52 minutes and 30 seconds. This means 7 night hours correspond to 8 daytime hours for working-time purposes.

Plan night schedules

Use the Datas Uteis schedule simulator to build night shifts and view team coverage.
